era_context: Mid-3rd century BCE. The Hellenistic world was dominated by the Successor States. The Second Syrian War was in its final years. Ashoka's Mauryan Empire was at its height. The First Punic War between Rome and Carthage was in its ninth year. China remained in the Warring States period.

events.title: First Punic War: Regulus's Invasion of North Africa
events.description: Following the Roman victory at Ecnomus in 256 BCE, the Roman general Marcus Atilius Regulus invaded North Africa with a substantial army. In 255 BCE, Regulus was campaigning in Tunisia, defeating Carthaginian forces and threatening Carthage itself. However, the Carthaginians hired the Spartan general Xanthippus, who reorganized their forces and defeated Regulus at the Battle of the Bagradas River (also called the Battle of Tunis). Regulus was captured and later executed. This campaign demonstrated Rome's ambition to strike at Carthage's homeland but also revealed the limits of Roman military power in unfamiliar terrain.
events.certainty_note: Regulus's invasion and defeat are well-attested in Roman historiography. The date 255 BCE is scholarly consensus. The campaign's significance is confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Ashoka's Continued Reign and Dhamma Propagation
events.description: In 255 BCE, Ashoka continued his reign as the Mauryan Emperor, maintaining his policy of Dhamma (Buddhist ethics) as the foundation of imperial governance. His edicts were inscribed across the empire, promoting non-violence, moral conduct, and Buddhist principles. Ashoka's reign was characterized by administrative efficiency, religious tolerance, and the spread of Buddhism. By this time, Ashoka had established himself as one of the most influential rulers of the ancient world, and his policies would have lasting effects on South Asian civilization.
events.certainty_note: Ashoka's edicts are contemporary epigraphic evidence. The dating to 255 BCE is approximate based on regnal chronology (traditional 268–232 BCE or revised 265–232 BCE).

events.title: Second Syrian War: Approaching Resolution
events.region: Eastern Mediterranean, Seleucid Empire, Ptolemaic Egypt
events.description: The Second Syrian War (260–253 BCE) was approaching its conclusion in 255 BCE. Both the Seleucid Empire and Ptolemaic Egypt were exhausted by years of conflict. The war would be resolved in 253 BCE through a marriage alliance between Antiochus II and Berenice II, daughter of Ptolemy II. This marriage was intended to cement peace, but it would later trigger the Third Syrian War when Antiochus II returned to his first wife, Laodice I.
events.certainty_note: The Second Syrian War's broad outline is well-attested, but specific events in 255 BCE are not independently documented. The war's conclusion in 253 BCE is scholarly consensus.

events.title: Qin Expansion Continues
events.description: The Qin state under King Zhao continued its military expansion and consolidation during the Warring States period. In 255 BCE, Qin was engaged in campaigns against neighboring states. The state's legalist administrative system and military innovations were giving it an advantage over rival states. The specific events of 255 BCE are not well-documented, but the broader trajectory of Qin expansion is clear.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled c. 109 BCE

events.certainty_note: Qin's expansion during this period is attested in Chinese historiography, but specific events in 255 BCE are not independently confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Bagradas River (where Regulus was defeated) is sometimes dated to 255 BCE and sometimes to 254 BCE in different sources. The exact location and details of the battle are debated by scholars. The Second Syrian War's specific events in 255 BCE are not independently documented. Qin's military campaigns in 255 BCE are not confirmed in contemporary sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 255 BCE is similar to previous years. Regulus's invasion and defeat are well-attested in Roman historiography, making this year relatively well-documented for the First Punic War. Ashoka's edicts provide contemporary evidence from South Asia. Chinese history relies entirely on the Shiji, compiled 150+ years after events. The surviving sources are biased toward elite political and military narratives.

graph_edges.note: Regulus's defeat at the Bagradas River led to his capture and later execution, and marked a turning point in the First Punic War
The Second Syrian War would conclude in 253 BCE with the marriage alliance between Antiochus II and Berenice II
